license: Curated paraphrase. OARSI 2019 / NICE NG226 / WHO content is publicly available; passages here are written by the Kneedle team for accessibility and lay-readability.

chunks.title: Land-based exercise is a core treatment
chunks.text: Regular land-based exercise (walking, strengthening, neuromuscular training) is strongly recommended for knee osteoarthritis. Benefits build over 4-8 weeks of consistent practice. Start gently; increase load only when pain stays under 5/10 during and after activity.

chunks.title: Strengthen the quadriceps
chunks.text: Quadriceps strengthening is a foundational treatment for knee OA. Isometric work (quad sets) is safe even when pain is high; progress to seated knee extensions and mini-squats as pain allows.

chunks.title: Walking dose
chunks.text: 30 minutes of moderate-pace walking on most days is appropriate for most people with mild-to-moderate knee OA. Split into 10-minute bouts if continuous walking provokes pain.

chunks.title: Aquatic exercise for severe pain
chunks.text: Aquatic (pool) exercise is conditionally recommended when land-based exercise is too painful. Water offloads about 70-90 percent of body weight at chest depth, allowing range-of-motion work without joint loading.

chunks.title: Weight loss helps load-bearing joints
chunks.text: Losing 5 to 10 percent of body weight measurably reduces knee pain and improves function in people with OA who are above their ideal weight. Each kilogram lost removes about four kilograms of force per step from the knee.

chunks.title: Sudden swelling plus fever is urgent
chunks.text: Sudden joint swelling, especially with fever or heat over the joint, can indicate infection (septic arthritis) and requires same-day medical assessment. This is not typical of osteoarthritis.

chunks.title: Pain at 8/10 or above persisting is a referral signal
chunks.text: OA pain that remains at 8/10 or above for several days despite rest and analgesia, or worsens steadily over a week, warrants prompt clinician review to reconsider treatment.

chunks.title: Heat and cold
chunks.text: Heat (15 minutes, warm not hot) helps stiff joints and warms muscles before exercise. Cold (15 minutes, wrapped) helps a swollen or freshly painful joint. Both are short-term symptom management, not a cure.

chunks.title: Knee sleeves and braces
chunks.text: Knee sleeves can reduce pain through warmth and proprioception. Unloader braces (for medial OA) shift load away from the painful compartment. A simple sleeve is reasonable to try; rigid braces should be fitted by a clinician.

chunks.title: When to refer to specialist services
chunks.text: Refer to a specialist if the person has joint symptoms that have a substantial impact on quality of life and are refractory to non-surgical treatment, if they cannot bear weight, if the knee locks or gives way frequently, or if there is sudden swelling with systemic symptoms.

chunks.title: OA is manageable, not curable
chunks.text: Osteoarthritis is a long-term condition managed by exercise, weight management, and symptom control. There is currently no medicine or supplement that reverses cartilage loss. Most people maintain a good quality of life with consistent self-management.

chunks.title: Topical NSAIDs first
chunks.text: For knee OA, topical NSAID gels (e.g. ibuprofen or diclofenac applied to the skin over the joint) are recommended before oral painkillers, because they reduce systemic side effects in older adults. Discuss with a pharmacist or doctor first.

chunks.title: Exercise is recommended regardless of severity
chunks.text: Therapeutic exercise should be offered as a core treatment for everyone with osteoarthritis, regardless of age, comorbidity, pain severity or disability. Benefits include reduced pain and improved physical function.

chunks.title: Aids and walking sticks
chunks.text: A walking stick used in the opposite hand to the painful knee can reduce knee load by 20-25 percent. It is a sign of self-care, not weakness, and is recommended for episodes of higher pain.

chunks.title: Nocturnal pain
chunks.text: Pain that wakes you at night, or pain when resting, is less typical of mechanical OA and should be discussed with a clinician. It may indicate inflammatory disease, a flare, or a need to reconsider treatment.

chunks.title: Locking or giving way needs a knee exam
chunks.text: A knee that locks (cannot fully straighten), catches, or gives way during walking suggests a mechanical problem such as a loose body or meniscal tear, distinct from OA itself. An in-person knee examination is recommended.

chunks.title: The two-hour rule
chunks.text: Exercise that causes pain above 5/10, or pain that lasts more than two hours after the session, is too intense. Reduce repetitions or range until the pain settles back to baseline before the next session.

chunks.title: Handling a flare
chunks.text: During a flare (sudden increase in pain), reduce exercise intensity but do not stop completely. Maintain isometric quad sets and gentle range-of-motion. Resume normal load gradually over 3-5 days.

chunks.title: OA prevalence in older adults
chunks.text: Osteoarthritis affects roughly 1 in 5 adults over 60 worldwide. Women are affected more than men. It is the single largest cause of mobility-related disability in older adults globally.

chunks.title: Mood and pain are linked
chunks.text: Low mood and sleep loss make OA pain feel worse. Treating depression, anxiety, and sleep difficulties is part of OA care, not separate from it.

chunks.title: Steroid injections - short-term relief
chunks.text: Intra-articular corticosteroid injections can give 2-4 weeks of pain relief during a severe flare, but provide no long-term benefit and may damage cartilage if repeated frequently. They are a tool, not a routine.

chunks.title: Glucosamine and chondroitin
chunks.text: OARSI gives no strong recommendation for glucosamine or chondroitin supplements for knee OA. Evidence is mixed and any benefit is small. They are safe to try if affordable, but do not replace exercise and weight management.

chunks.title: Footwear matters
chunks.text: Cushioned, well-fitted walking shoes reduce knee impact at heel strike. Heels above 3 cm and worn-out soles increase knee load and should be avoided during flares.

chunks.title: Asymmetric gait protects the painful knee but loads the other
chunks.text: Limping or favouring one knee shifts load to the other side, which over months can accelerate OA in the previously healthy knee. Symmetry-focused gait retraining is a recognised treatment.

chunks.title: Lateral trunk lean reduces knee load
chunks.text: Leaning the trunk slightly toward the painful side during stance phase reduces the knee adduction moment by 10-20 percent. It is a known compensatory pattern; a physiotherapist can teach it deliberately when appropriate.
